subject: Kitchen remodels: decide these while walls are open
preheader: Induction, ventilation, lighting, and the insulation window you only get once
title: The energy choices inside a kitchen remodel
introHtml: A kitchen remodel is the one time your busiest room gets stripped to the studs. With heating season arriving on the Cape, it's worth asking before demo day: what does energy efficiency mean to you? Lower bills, a warmer floor in January, cleaner air while you cook? Your answer should shape the plan.
articleHtml:
Five decisions that set how your kitchen performs for decades

The cooktop. Induction heats the pan directly through a magnetic field, so almost no heat spills into the room — the burner is cool seconds after the pot comes off. Less waste heat also means your hood has less to clear. If you're considering it, have the electrical panel checked early; running a new circuit is simple while walls are open and disruptive after.

Ventilation. A hood should duct outside, not recirculate — grease and moisture belong outdoors, not in your cabinets. Shorter, straighter duct runs move more air with less noise. And bigger isn't better: an oversized hood in a tight house can pull air backward down chimneys and flues, so sizing is a real design decision.

Lighting. Layer LEDs — general, under-cabinet, task — on separate switches so you only light what you're using. Ask for airtight, IC-rated recessed fixtures; ordinary cans punch leaky holes into the ceiling that bleed heated air all winter.

Appliance placement. Keep the refrigerator away from the range and out of direct sun, and leave clearance around its coils. A fridge fighting a hot neighbor runs constantly.

The one-time opportunity

Open walls are the cheapest moment your house will ever have to air-seal and insulate. Exterior walls, the band joist below the floor, gaps around pipes and wiring — all reachable for a few days, then buried for 30 years. See how we approach kitchen updates and energy-smart remodeling, or read more on our blog.
tips: Not sure about induction? If a magnet sticks to the bottom of your current pots, they'll work on an induction cooktop.
Before drywall closes, photograph every open wall — you'll know exactly where pipes, wires, and blocking live forever after.
Test your hood with a piece of paper: if it won't hold flat against the running hood, the duct run or fan needs attention.
